Striking patterns: fully cross-spectrum adoption — LFI attacks the frame while using it (Martin 2023-11-21: "Vous prétendez être juste au motif que..."), Écologistes use it ironically (Lucas-Lundy 2023-12-11: "quelques régularisations... pour quelques métiers en tension"), RN engages it on its own terms (Cousin 2023-11-27), and it persists post-law in AES/regularization debates through 2026 (Berger 2026-05-12 on the 23 Jan 2025 instructions; anonymous 2026-06-23 invoking "la loi qu'avait fait adopter Gérald Darmanin"). Also notable: Stella Dupont, an author of the 2018 regularization-experiment amendment, prefigures the exact mechanism the formula later names — an intra-majority genealogy rather than an opposition capture. The role tag "counter" is apt post-2022 (economic counter-framing to identity-based immigration framing) but the 2018 background is neutral-technical.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2019-01-30 — unidentified hearing participant (auditioned expert/witness), Commission d'enquête sur la lutte contre les groupuscules d'extrême droite, explicitly discussing Renaud Camus's theory. First confirmed politician: Christophe Castaner (LREM, ministre de l'Intérieur), 2019-05-09, same CE. First in séance publique: Gisèle Biémouret (Socialistes), 2019-10-07, immigration-policy debate — used to refute the theory.
birth_notes: Expected birth (Camus 2010, mainstreamed by Zemmour 2021) predates the corpus, so no pre-birth idiomatic background exists — unlike phrases such as "quoi qu'il en coûte", "grand remplacement" has essentially no ordinary-idiom life; every corpus occurrence descends from Camus. The phrase enters the AN in January 2019, two-plus years BEFORE Zemmour's 2021 campaign, and not as a claim but as an object of investigation: the CE on far-right groupuscules (Jan-May 2019), with the Christchurch attack (March 2019) as the visible trigger — multiple 2019 quotes cite Christchurch explicitly. Its introducers are hearing witnesses and then the interior minister (Castaner), i.e. the state apparatus naming a threat ideology, not a party adopting a slogan. In the entire sample, NO speaker sincerely asserts the immigration-sense formula in the first person: it is always quoted, attributed ("la théorie du", "les thèses du", "prétendu"), refuted, or used as an accusation against opponents (Balanant to a witness, Stella Dupont "votre appropriation des thèses"). The hémicycle taboo posited in expected_birth holds throughout 2017-2026.
notes: 38 unique quotes audited (15 first + 30 random, 7 overlapping). 0 false positives — the two patterns are precise as-is; "remplacis(me|te|tes)" produced no sampled match and no FP evidence (it misses the rare plural "remplacismes", but that gap is negligible and zero-risk, so no change proposed). The 7 ambiguous cases are all deliberate ironic TRANSPOSITIONS of the formula to other domains, concentrated post-2024: "grand remplacement du boeuf francais par le boeuf canadien" (Alfandari, Horizons, CETA 2024-05-30), Musk's EVs (Michoux, Union des Droites, 2025-03-04), "grand remplacement des addictions" (2025-11-07), Chinese industry/EVs x3 (2025-12-17 QaG + seance, 2026-01-06), and Chudeau (RN) uttering it only via Luc Ferry's AI book title (2026-04-07). These are memetic offspring, not lexical accidents — keep them matched; they are analytically valuable: right-of-center speakers recycle the template "grand remplacement de X par Y" (especially anti-China/anti-Green-Deal), preserving the formula's charge while dodging the immigration taboo — a dog-whistle displacement pattern. Venue pattern: born and incubated in commissions d'enquete/missions (groupuscules 2019, racisme 2020, principes de la Republique 2021, ideologie islamiste 2025); first seance publique use 2019-10-07. Usage is overwhelmingly left/center/government denouncing the theory; RN itself appears only via transposition or citation. Recommend the analysis tag the transposed-domain cluster separately (e.g. a post-hoc "transposition" flag on quotes matching "grand remplacement (industriel|des vehicules|du boeuf|des addictions)") rather than excluding it at regex level.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2018-03-21 — Clémentine Autain (LFI), Commission des affaires étrangères, audition of Interior Minister Gérard Collomb: "Ma seconde question porte sur ce que certaines associations appellent le délit de solidarité." Collomb responds using the term the same day.
birth_notes: Corpus is consistent with the expected story but sharpens it: the phrase enters the AN on 2018-03-21 via LFI (Autain), explicitly credited to the associative milieu ("ce que certaines associations appellent"), ~7 months after the expected GISTI/Herrou birth (2017-08) and exactly when the loi asile-immigration reaches committee. The minister himself (Collomb) engages the term the same day to contest it, and by 2018-07-25 concedes "ce qu'il est désormais convenu d'appeler le délit de solidarité" — a textbook adoption arc. The formula is however far older than the 2017 expected birth: in-corpus, Karamanli dates the offense to 1938 (modified 2003, 2012), Valls recalls his own 2012 partial abrogation of "ce que l'on appelle le délit de solidarité", and Boudié notes the abrogation debate "s'était déjà posée en 2012" — matching GISTI's real coinage in the mid-1990s and the 2003 "délinquants de la solidarité" manifesto. No pre-2018 background exists in the corpus itself (which starts 2017), so no idiomatic baseline to flag.
notes: Zero false positives across all 36 unique quotes (15 first + 30 random, 9 overlapping) — "délit de solidarité" has no competing literal sense, making this one of the cleanest phrases in the set. Pattern 2 ("delinquants (solidaires|de la solidarite)") yielded no sampled matches but is the attested 2003 GISTI companion formula; keep it. Striking usage dynamics: (1) near-universal distancing markers ("ce que l'on appelle", "improprement", "surnomme", "fameux", "drôle d'oxymore" — Victory even names it an oxymoron), showing the formula itself is what is being fought over; (2) it is a left/center counter-frame — LFI, PS, PCF, Écologistes sincere; LaREM/MoDem adopt it while denying its legal existence ("n'existe pas en droit", Saulignac concedes; Moutchou "improprement"); no RN or LR occurrence in any sample; (3) venue is overwhelmingly Commission des lois, with intensity spikes tied to the 2018 asylum-immigration bill, the 2018-07-06 Conseil constitutionnel fraternité decision (cited repeatedly), and a long tail through the 2025 immigration debates; (4) 2022 Sebaihi quote re-anchors it to the Cédric Herrou/Roya origin story, confirming the associative genealogy stays attached to the phrase.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2018-07-11, Élise Fajgeles (Renaissance, rapporteure), Commission des lois, nouvelle lecture of the asile-immigration law — 5 days after the Conseil constitutionnel decision of 2018-07-06; Florent Boudié follows in the same session with three more uses.
birth_notes: The corpus confirms the expected birth with unusual precision: zero matches from 2017 through early July 2018, then first use on 2018-07-11 explicitly citing the fresh CC decision. There is NO pre-birth idiomatic background — 'principe de fraternité' effectively did not exist as parliamentary language before the Conseil constitutionnel created it, making this one of the cleanest juridically-born formulas in the corpus. Introduced in the AN by the Renaissance majority (Fajgeles, Boudié) defensively — claiming their délit de solidarité reform had 'anticipated' the principle — then immediately weaponized by the left (Untermaier/PS, Peu/PCF) to demand full abrogation of the délit de solidarité. The right enters in critique by 2019-02-21 (Ciotti: the decision 'complique redoutablement la lutte contre les réseaux de passeurs'). RN appears only from 2025, in gouvernement-des-juges framing (Sophie Blanc: 'créé du droit constitutionnel sans l'aval du peuple') or ironic appropriation (Monnier 2026).
notes: False-positive rate is 0% — the phrase has no literal other-sense, so patterns need no fixes; the second pattern (valeur constitutionnelle de la fraternite) contributed no visible noise. The 7 ambiguous quotes are generic devise-triptych rhetoric (liberté/égalité/fraternité restated as 'principes') in non-immigration venues: bioethics (2019-08-29), education (Blanquer 2019-10-23), sécurité sociale/CNR (Molac 2024 x2), outre-mer justice (Califer 2025), fin de vie (2026-02-09), and Monnier/RN (2026-06-26). These are diffusion signal, not noise — the constitutional consecration plausibly re-energized devise rhetoric — so I recommend keeping them rather than excluding by venue. Striking patterns: (1) role 'counter' is validated — the formula is overwhelmingly invoked by left/center AGAINST restrictive immigration measures, while the right/RN only cite it to attack the Conseil constitutionnel; (2) venue is heavily Commission des lois, with resurgence peaks tracking immigration bills (2018 asile-immigration, 2021 séparatisme/contrat d'engagement républicain where the formula entered statute text, 2023 loi immigration); (3) the formula migrated from immigration into other domains over time (bioethics, laïcité, fin de vie), a classic legalism-to-rhetoric trajectory. Counts are over 33 distinct quotes (15 first-in-corpus + 18 non-duplicate random samples; 12 random samples duplicated first-15 entries).

true_birth_in_corpus: 2019-01-30, unidentified speaker (Commission d'enquête sur la lutte contre les groupuscules d'extrême droite): "c'est la théorie de la « remigration »" — 3+ years before the expected Zemmour birth (2022-03-22). First confirmed-deputy use: Marie-France Lorho (RN), 2021-03-17, commission des lois — and it is a DISAVOWAL of the term, still pre-Zemmour.
birth_notes: The corpus contradicts the expected birth story in an interesting way: "remigration" appears in AN discourse from January 2019 — over three years before Zemmour's "ministère de la remigration" (2022-03-22) — but exclusively in the 2019 commission d'enquête on far-right groupuscules, where witnesses and deputies describe the identitarian (Génération Identitaire / Renaud Camus) theory and quote militant slogans ("moins de régularisation, plus de remigration"). So the term entered parliament via its OPPONENTS documenting the far right, not via adopters. There is no pre-birth ordinary-idiom background: the word has no everyday French sense in this corpus (the demographic "return migration" sense never occurs). Crucially, across all 11 matches the term is NEVER used sincerely as advocated policy — the sole right-wing utterance (Lorho, RN, 2021-03-17) explicitly distances her amendment from "la « remigration »". Post-2022 uses are all left-wing denunciations (Portes LFI 2023, Panot LFI 2025 re AfD, QAG 2026) or expert analysis of Trump/Orbán/AfD platforms (affaires étrangères 2024). In the AN, remigration is a repoussoir wielded against the far right, not an adopted talking point — the inverse of the usual élément-de-langage lifecycle, and worth stating explicitly in the analysis given the phrase's "successor" role in the immigration-cadrage family.
notes: Corpus contains only 11 matches total (first_15 and random_samples are the same exhaustive set), all genuine; the bare pattern remigrations? is precise since the word has no competing literal sense in parliamentary French, so no exclusions or tightening needed. One data-quality note, not a pattern issue: the 2026-01-21 quote ("Un site qui parle de remigration, de défrancisation et de grand remplacement.") is captured twice from the same QAG moment under two session records (session_id 18097972 "Questions au gouvernement" and 18096546 "1ère séance" séance publique) — dedup at the session/quote level would avoid double-counting in frequency curves. Venue pattern: 2019-2024 usage lives almost entirely in commissions (CE groupuscules, lois, affaires étrangères); it only reaches the hémicycle/QAG in 2025-2026, consistent with the term's mainstreaming after Zemmour 2022 and the AfD "remigration" scandal of early 2024.

fp_examples: Par ailleurs, je préside l’association Premiers de cordée, qui encourage la pratique sportive dans les services pédiatriques des hôpitaux.

pattern_fixes.detail: association premiers de cordee — proper noun of a real charity (sport in pediatric hospitals) that surfaces in sports-federation hearings; excluding the exact name costs zero genuine political coverage

true_birth_in_corpus: 2017-10-17 — Olivier Faure (Socialistes), séance publique (QAG / PLF 2018 discussion générale), two days after Macron's 2017-10-15 TF1/LCI interview; two consecutive quotes inverting the formula ("les premiers de cordée ne peuvent être les plus fortunés")
birth_notes: Corpus matches the expected birth story with one twist: the formula is born OUTSIDE the AN (Macron TV interview 2017-10-15) and is imported into the chamber by the OPPOSITION, not the majority — Olivier Faure (PS) 48h later, already as a contested/inverted quotation. There is zero pre-birth idiomatic background in the corpus (no ordinary alpinism/idiom uses before 2017-10-15), so the phrase is a clean political creation from the corpus's point of view. Sincere majority usage is nearly absent throughout; the sole Renaissance use sampled (Melchior 2021-04-14) already redirects it to underpaid essential workers rather than the wealthy.
notes: Usage profile: this is an OPPOSITION-OWNED irony marker, not a government talking point — dominated by PS/GDR-PCF/LFI, joined by LR (Dive 2021, Viry 2022) and RN (Tanguy 2022, Masson 2022), almost always in guillemets or with distancing ("ceux que vous osez appeler", "le mythe de", "vous vous en souvenez ?"), tied to ISF suppression and 'ruissellement'. Second life from 2020: Covid-era reappropriation toward essential workers (agriculteurs, soignants, caissières, fret ferroviaire, chauffeurs-livreurs Uber), frequently colliding with the sibling counter-formulas "premiers de tranchée" (Faure), "premiers de corvée" (Macron's own riff, quoted by Dufour 2022) and "première ligne" — worth cross-referencing those phrases in the same subject family. Striking late finding: RN's Casterman (2026-05-27) uses the formula SINCERELY in its original Macronian sense, applied to farmers. The 3 ambiguous cases are all post-2023 bleached "leading the way" uses (incl. minister Laurence Boone), evidence the formula is dissolving back into ordinary idiom in its final years — an interesting endpoint for the genealogy. Venue pattern: séance publique dominates the ironic-attack uses; commissions host the reappropriations and the bleached uses. Only structural FP is the charity name (excluded via fix). First-15 list and first 15 random samples are identical — effective unique sample is 30 quotes.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2020-04-22 — Jean-Michel Mis (LREM / Renaissance), Commission des lois: "Les maires, devenus bien souvent « premiers de corvée »…" — first AN occurrence, 2.5 years after Mélenchon's public coinage, and notably from the majority camp the formula was coined against. First use by the originating left: François Ruffin (LFI), 2020-05-27.
birth_notes: Expected birth (Mélenchon, 2017-10-16, TF1 counter-formula to Macron's "premiers de cordée") is NOT in the corpus and the phrase has zero AN presence 2017-2019 — no ordinary-idiom background exists because it is a pure coinage with no prior French idiom sense. The corpus confirms the "seconde vie Covid" thesis exactly: first AN use is 2020-04-22, and strikingly by a LREM deputy (Jean-Michel Mis, in scare quotes, about mayors), i.e. the majority voicing the opposition's counter-formula before any LFI member does in the AN (Ruffin follows 2020-05-27). Covid 2020-21 sees genuine cross-spectrum adoption (LREM Motin uses it sincerely with the bas/haut framing; Agir, MoDem, LIOT also use it); post-Covid it re-narrows to the left (LFI, GDR, PS, Ecolos) and migrates to new fights: 2023 retraites (Tellier, Panot), RATP drivers (Sebaihi 2023), housing/AESH (2025). Bonus finding: Roger Vicot (PS, 2024-03-06) misattributes the phrase to Macron himself ("le Président de la République avait joliment qualifié ces personnes… de « premiers de corvée »") — an ironic inversion of the genealogy.
notes: Pattern quality is excellent: "premier(e?s)? de corvees?" has no competing literal sense (unlike réarmement/submersion), so 0/21 false positives; the optional plural on "corvees?" correctly catches Bruneel's "premiers de corvées" (2021-11-10) and the feminine "premières de corvée" (Fiat, Motin, Goulet) is caught by "premiere?s". No exclusions needed. Analytical texture worth keeping: heavy meta-marking in early uses (guillemets, "ceux qu'on a appelés", "ces fameux", "dits") shows speakers citing a recognized formula; the phrase functions as a durable category label for low-paid essential workers well beyond Covid.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2017-10-11 — Ericka Bareigts (Socialistes, commission des affaires sociales, audition de Buzyn et Darmanin): "La théorie du ruissellement vaudrait-elle pour la richesse et pas pour la pauvreté ?"
birth_notes: The corpus antedates the expected birth story by 4 days: Bareigts (PS) uses the fully-formed talking point in commission on 2017-10-11, before the 2017-10-15 Macron TF1 interview usually credited with crystallizing the formula ("premiers de cordée" response). So the phrase was already circulating as an anti-Macron attack line inside the AN during the PLF 2018 / ISF-suppression debate before the famous public TV moment. There is no ordinary-idiom pre-political background in the corpus — the patterns are specific enough that no literal (hydrological) "ruissellement" appears at all. The second corpus user is, strikingly, a majority MP (Cesarini, LREM, 2017-11-29) applying the formula analytically to territorial policy — the earliest sign of the formula's domain extension beyond fiscal policy.
notes: Zero false positives across 38 unique quotes — the anchor-word patterns (theorie/politique/mythe/dogme/fable du ruissellement, fameux ruissellement, crois pas au ruissellement, ruissellement des richesses) fully exclude literal water-runoff usage, which in AN debates phrases as "eaux de ruissellement" and never matches. The 5 AMBIGUOUS quotes are a coherent sub-cluster, not noise: the EGalim/SRP+10 sense of "ruissellement" (price gains trickling down the agri-food chain to farmers) — 3 quotes from the 2019 grande-distribution commission d'enquête (auditioned witnesses incl. Michel-Édouard Leclerc), plus Allegret-Pilot (UDR, 2025-03-17) and an agriculture commission quote (2026-01-21). Same formula, different referent — keep them, but analysts should segment this agri-food branch. Analytical findings: (1) pure counter-formula — no sincere first-person claimant anywhere; the only Renaissance uses are ironic or defensive (Beaudouin-Hubiere teasing a socialist for "supporting" it; Saint-Martin 2021: "vous êtes seul à parler" of it — explicitly confirming the expected-birth thesis inside the corpus itself); (2) exceptional cross-spectrum adoption: PS, LFI, GDR/PCF, Écologistes, LR, RN, UDR all wield it; (3) lifecycle: born in commissions 2017-2019, migrates to séance publique, intensifies sharply 2023-2026 in budget/superprofits debates with escalating variants ("mythe du ruissellement", "fumeuse théorie", "politique du ruissellement", "le fameux ruissellement !"), plus running gags about waiting for "la première goutte"; (4) domain extensions: territories/métropoles (2017-2019, used even by LR), agri-food prices (2019-2026), housing (2025). Random-sample overlap: 7 of the 30 random samples duplicate first-15 quotes; counts above are on unique quotes.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2017-12-07 — unidentified hearing witness (journalists J.-M. Quatrepoint / P. Gateaud) before the commission d'enquête on industrial policy ("Il serait un leurre de penser que la France va devenir une « Start-up Nation » comme Israël"). First elected speaker: Olivier Marleix (LR), 2018-06-29, commission des lois, in derisive use ("le cabinet d'audit de la start-up nation").
birth_notes: The public birth (Macron, April 2017 campaign / VivaTech June 2017, borrowed from the 2009 book on Israel) predates all corpus matches, so no pre-birth idiomatic baseline exists here. The corpus adds a twist to the birth story inside the AN: the phrase enters via commission d'enquête witnesses, not deputies — journalists (Dec 2017) and economists Cohen/Veltz (Jan 2018, already mocking it as "inflation verbale"), with Bpifrance's Dufourcq (Feb 2018) providing the only sincere early uses (Israeli model, French Tech "coq rouge"). The first deputy use (Marleix, LR, June 2018) is already ironic: in parliament the majority's slogan lives almost entirely as an opposition weapon (LR, LFI, RN, PS, Ruffin), with rare Renaissance uses being ironic counter-jabs (Bothorel's "chief sadness officer" 2020; Panosyan-Bouvet 2024). Bonus meta-quote: Colombier (RN, 2023-05-10) explicitly calls it "éléments de langage".
notes: 45 quote entries read; 38 unique after removing 7 duplicates between first-15 and random samples. All genuine — "start-up nation" is a proper-noun coinage with no competing literal sense in this corpus, so the empty exclude list is correct. Patterns already handle hyphen/space/no-space variants ("start-up nation", "start-up Nation", "startup nation" all matched in samples); "nation start-up" pattern showed no matches but costs nothing. Early Israel-referencing matches are the formula's source sense debated as France's benchmark, i.e. genuine debate-about-the-formula, not false positives. Usage is stable 2017-2026, overwhelmingly ironic/critical, commissions first then séance publique from late 2019.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2021-05-06 — Clémentine Autain (LFI), séance publique on 'RSA pour les jeunes de 18 à 25 ans': « Non, il ne suffit pas de traverser la rue pour trouver un emploi. »
birth_notes: Expected birth (2018-09-15, Macron to a young horticulteur) happened OUTSIDE Parliament, and Macron never speaks in the corpus — so the AN life of this phrase is 100% oppositional appropriation; the 'author' of the formula has zero corpus occurrences. First corpus match is 2021-05-06 (Autain, LFI), a ~2.5-year lag after the public birth; no pre-birth idiomatic background appears (patterns are employment-anchored, so ordinary literal 'traverser la rue' is excluded by design). The 2018-2020 silence is suspicious — QAG echoes in autumn 2018 would be expected — worth verifying whether the corpus actually covers 2018-2020 sessions or whether coverage effectively starts ~2021. Introduced in the AN by the left (LFI/PCF/Écologistes dominate), adopted by LR in Feb 2022 (Benassaya) and RN in May 2023 (Levavasseur), i.e. a fully trans-partisan anti-Macron marker.
notes: 28 unique quotes audited (first-15 list + 13 non-duplicate random samples). Zero clear false positives; the two AMBIGUOUS cases are both plausible allusions caught in non-employment contexts: Saint-Hubert 2025-05-21 ('Il suffit de traverser la rue, aller ouvrir un centre de soins non programmés' — literal street-crossing by hospital doctors, possibly sarcastic echo; caught by bare pattern 2) and Leseul 2026-06-30 ('Ils ne peuvent plus traverser la rue... par rapport à l'emploi' — likely deliberate inversion of the formula about the very poor, but literal reading possible). Patterns kept as-is: tightening pattern 2 with a work-word requirement would cost genuine detached uses for a ~3.6% ambiguity rate. Striking usage facts: (1) 100% ironic/negated/quotation usage — never a sincere endorsement, typically 'il ne suffit pas de...' or explicit attribution ('pour reprendre les mots du Président de la République'); (2) frequently bundled with Macron's other petites phrases ('pognon de dingue', 'costard', 'ceux qui ne sont rien', 'Gaulois réfractaires') as a mépris-d'en-haut litany (Panot 2022, Diaz/RN 2023, Vannier 2023); (3) inverted weaponization exists (Boyard 2023-11-28: 'encore un macroniste qui sait traverser la rue pour trouver du travail'); (4) by 2025-2026 the phrase appears from unidentified speakers in proverbial detached form ('comme disait l'autre', 'un an pour traverser une rue ça fait beaucoup') — fully lexicalized in AN discourse; (5) venue split roughly half séance publique / half commission, with Affaires sociales the epicenter (plein-emploi / assurance-chômage bills). Louis Boyard (LFI) is the heaviest single user in the sample (4 quotes). Known recall limitation (not an FP issue): pattern 1 requires the work word AFTER the phrase within 80 chars, so 'trouver un travail en traversant la rue' word orders are missed.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2018-06-27 — Sébastien Jumel (GDR / Communistes), Commission des lois: "En France, un « pognon de dingue » serait dépensé, dit-on, pour la solidarité." First AN occurrence, 15 days after Macron's 2018-06-12 video, already ironic quotation against the author.
birth_notes: Corpus is fully consistent with the expected birth: zero occurrences before Macron's 2018-06-12 video — no pre-birth idiomatic background whatsoever (the vulgarism never appeared in parliamentary debate before), which makes this an unusually clean marker phrase. Crucially, the phrase is introduced into the AN not by Macron's camp but by the communist opposition (Jumel, GDR, 2018-06-27 in commission; then Dufrègne, GDR, 2018-07-12 in séance publique), quoting the president's words against him. The formula's entire parliamentary life is oppositional/ironic: nobody on the majority side ever uses it sincerely in its original anti-welfare sense — majority speakers only engage defensively or by negation (Jolivet/Horizons 2023: "je ne prétends pas qu'un pognon de dingue est dépensé"; Benoit/Horizons 2026: "ça fait pas un pognon de dingue").
notes: 38 unique quotes judged (15 first-in-corpus + 23 non-duplicate random samples). Precision is effectively 100%: "pognon de dingue" has no literal other-sense, so the single bare pattern with no exclusions is correct — do not add exclusions. The one AMBIGUOUS case (2025-09-03, commission d'enquête santé mentale, unidentified speaker — likely a hearing witness: "ça coûte un pognon de dingue, j'allais dire") is plain vernacular rather than a deliberate Macron echo, itself evidence of the phrase's normalization. Striking patterns for the analysis: (1) systematic REVERSAL — the opposition redirects the formula at spending Macron favors: "assistanat des riches" (Chassaigne 2018), "assistés du CAC40" (Simonnet 2023), cabinets de conseil (Fernandes 2022, Lucas-Lundy 2024, Simonnet 2024), exonérations de cotisations (Guedj/Erodi 2024), niches fiscales, nucléaire, JO; (2) attribution decay — 2018-2022 almost always in quotation marks with explicit attribution ("comme dirait notre Président", "expression chère à M. Macron"), 2024+ increasingly bare and unattributed, fully absorbed into parliamentary vernacular; (3) late RN adoption in 2025 (Guitton 2025-02-19, Marais-Beuil 2025-06-18) with the target flipped onto left-wing spending — a double reversal; (4) venue: heavy commission usage, especially Finances and Affaires sociales during budget/PLFSS debates; (5) user base starts GDR/LFI-exclusive (2018-2022) then spreads to Écologistes, PS, Horizons, RN — full cross-spectrum diffusion by 2025.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2022-09-26 — Laure Lavalette (RN), commission des affaires sociales (audition Braun/Combe/Attal, PLFSS 2023): first AN occurrence, already an ironic détournement of Macron's 2022-08-24 formula ("la fin de l'abondance pour nos compatriotes, c'est non ; la fin de l'opulence que vous offrez à ceux venus d'ailleurs, c'est oui !")
birth_notes: Corpus fully corroborates the expected birth story: zero occurrences of "fin de l'abondance" (or "l'abondance et l'insouciance") anywhere in 2017–mid-2022, so there is no ordinary-idiom background — Macron genuinely coined the formula into political discourse on 2022-08-24 (Conseil des ministres, outside the corpus). The first parliamentary carrier is NOT the majority but the RN (Lavalette, 33 days later), immediately reframing it against immigration. The phrase then lives in the AN almost entirely as an opposition boomerang: LFI dominates (Bompard x3, Ruffin, Legrain, Chauche, Lachaud = 7/15), plus Écologistes (Sas, Belluco, Batho, Peytavie), Socialistes (Thomin), LR (Di Filippo), RN (Lavalette). The ONLY government-side use in four years is Christophe Béchu (2023-11-21), sincerely repurposing it for water scarcity/drought — the majority never adopts its own president's formula as a talking point in parliament. Late signal: Lachaud 2026-05-04 recycles it into the military-programmation/war-economy "effort et sacrifice" frame.
notes: The random_samples block is an exact duplicate of first_15_in_corpus, so the corpus contains only 15 matches total — this audit covers 100% of matches. Zero false positives: every match is meta-referential, ironic, or sincere reuse of the Macron formula; "abondance" never appears in a literal/other sense. Pattern health: "fin de l'abondance" carries 14/15 hits; "l'abondance et (de )?l'insouciance" carries exactly 1 (Peytavie's ironic inversion, genuine); "fin de l'insouciance" carries 0 hits in the corpus — dead weight but harmless, no fix needed. Venue pattern: heavily commission-based (10/15), concentrated in budget contexts (PLF/PLFSS 2023) in the two months after the birth; usage collapses after Feb 2023 with only three late echoes (Peytavie Oct 2023, Béchu Nov 2023, Lachaud May 2026). Usage mode: overwhelmingly ironic/quotation-marked (guillemets, "la fameuse", "on entend dire") — a textbook case of a presidential élément de langage that survives in parliament only as ammunition against its author.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2019-06-12 — Guillaume Larrivé (LR), commission des lois ("Réarmer la nation !", shouted twice in the same session) — 4.5 years before Macron's expected 2024-01-16 birth. As a GOVERNMENT talking point, the in-corpus birth is Amélie de Montchalin 2021-06-01 ("réarmement de l'État territorial"), consolidated by Bruno Le Maire 2021-09-22 ("réarmement régalien", PLF 2022) and Gabriel Attal 2022-10-14 ("réarmement de nos services publics").
birth_notes: The expected birth (Macron, 2024-01-16 press conference) is NOT the birth of the formula in the AN — it is the point where an already-circulating formula got generalized and presidentialized. Corpus genealogy: (1) 2019 — right-wing slogan: Larrivé (LR) interjects "Réarmer la nation !" during the internal-security bill, pure political-slogan use. (2) 2020-21 — cross-spectrum metaphorical spread: PS ("réarmer le pays", Hutin; "réarmement économique", Potier), GDR/communistes ("réarmement de l'État", Dufrègne), LREM ("réarmement budgétaire", Chouat). (3) 2021 — the Macron government itself adopts it as budget framing: Montchalin "réarmement de l'État territorial" (June 2021, official Interior-ministry language), Le Maire "réarmement régalien" (Sept 2021, PLF 2022 — repeated by Cazeneuve and Lefèvre in Sept 2022), Attal "réarmement de nos services publics" (Oct 2022), Guerini (Nov 2023). So Macron's Jan 2024 "réarmement démographique/civique" landed on ground his own ministers had tilled since mid-2021. (4) Notable pre-2024 signal: Poussier-Winsback (Horizons, Dec 2022) explicitly labels "réarmement moral" part of "le champ lexical du Rassemblement national" — the formula was perceived as far-right-coded before the president adopted it; Gassilloud (Dec 2021) quotes the SGDSN's "réarmement moral et psychologique" (state-resilience discourse). None of this pre-2024 background is ordinary idiom — it is already political usage, i.e. genuine precursor lineage, not noise.
notes: FP rate 0/42 — the adjective/complement-anchored patterns fully block literal military-rearmament matches; even "réarmement régalien … en faveur de nos armées" (Cazeneuve 2022) is the budget-framing metaphor. Usage patterns worth surfacing in the analysis: after Jan 2024, "réarmement démographique" is overwhelmingly used in hostile quotation — LFI and Écologistes (Panot twice, Garin, Maximi, Boyard twice, Peytavie) wield it ironically with recurring Viktor Orbán comparisons, while sincere use survives on the right (Bazin, Liégeon) and in natalist missions ("Baisse de la natalité" mission, 2025, treats it as an adopted technical term: "ce qu'on a appelé le réarmement démographique"). By contrast "réarmement industriel" is used sincerely across the whole spectrum, including a communist (Maurel 2024). Venue: pre-2024 occurrences are almost all in commissions (esp. Finances); post-2024 the phrase moves into séance publique. RN barely appears as a user in the sample (only Ménagé, and in a jab at a Renaissance minister) — the far-right connotation is asserted by opponents more than demonstrated by RN usage in this corpus. Optional (not required) widening: Maximi's quote lists "réarmement académique" among Macron's variants — adding academique to the adjective alternation (rearmement (demographique|civique|academique|…)) would be zero-FP-risk; bare "rearmement" should NOT be added (would flood with literal military uses). Data hygiene note: party_name "Les Récuplicains" is a typo for "Les Républicains" in the corpus metadata.

fp_examples: Cette disposition vise à provoquer un choc d’offre en matière de logement et à libérer du foncier.
Afin de rétablir l’égalité des chances, il faudra sans doute un « choc d’offre » sur l’accueil de ces enfants dans les structures collectives.
Pourtant, nous faisons face d'abord à un choc d'offre importé : guerre au Proche-Orient, fermeture du détroit d'Ormuz et flambée des prix de l'énergie.

pattern_fixes.detail: chocs? d'offre — of its ~10 sample matches, all but one are other-sense: sectoral supply boosts (logement/foncier: Pupponi 2017-10-11/19, Jolivet, Vuilletet, Letchimy; accueil d'enfants: Lang 2018; train: Tellier 2024, Bonnet 2026) or the textbook macro 'supply shock' ('choc d'offre négatif' Rousseau 2025, 'choc d'offre importé' Renault 2026). Not the supply-side-doctrine talking point.
chocs? de l'offre — same contamination: 'choc de l'offre' housing-norms irony (Panot 2018) and 'choc de l'offre' terrains à bâtir (2025-10-20). Zero clean doctrine hits in the sample.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2017-07-04 — Jean-Luc Mélenchon (LFI), séance publique, in reply to Édouard Philippe's déclaration de politique générale: "Nous n'avons que faire d'une politique de l'offre et de la contraction de la demande."
birth_notes: Expected birth (Hollande's January 2014 press-conference consecration of a 1980s term) predates the corpus, which starts 2017-07 — so the corpus cannot show the birth itself, only the established baseline, consistent with the phrase's declared "baseline" role. Two findings anyway: (1) the term enters the AN corpus on the very first day of the legislature (2017-07-04) in the mouth of its OPPONENT — Mélenchon — and for the first four months it is exclusively an LFI critique label (Mélenchon, Coquerel x3, Quatennens, Panot); (2) the first sincere pro-side claim is Bruno Le Maire on 2017-11-16 ("une politique qui est résolument une politique de l'offre"), who remains its main government voice through 2025. The doctrine phrase thus lives in the corpus primarily as a hostile exonym that the government only occasionally reclaims.
notes: Sample = 15 first + 30 random = 45 quotes. FP rate 12/45 ≈ 27%, entirely attributable to the two "choc" patterns; the core pattern "politiques? de l'offre" is 100% clean in this sample (31/31 genuine, including meta-debate uses like Martinet 2023 defining the term and Loubet/RN 2025 denying the label applies to France). "economie de l'offre" produced no sampled matches — harmless to keep. The existing excludes (offre de soins/logement/formation/transport) remain useful for "politique de l'offre de soins"-type phrases and should stay. The 2 ambiguous: Pupponi 2017-11-29 ("choc d'offre avec des réductions fiscales" — fiscal but housing-device context) and Panot 2018-04-17 (ironic quotation of the government's housing "choc de l'offre"). Cost of dropping the choc patterns is ~1 borderline-genuine match (Battistel 2024 "échecs du choc d'offre… choc de la demande", itself housing-sectoral) — an excellent trade. Striking patterns: usage is heavily asymmetric (LFI/Socialistes/GDR/Écologistes wield it as an accusation; government uses it rarely but assertively — Le Maire 2017/2022/2025, Lescure 2026 defending it as "très fortement décriée"); venue is overwhelmingly Commission des finances plus the 2025 commissions d'enquête (réindustrialisation, licenciements) where the doctrine's retrospective "bilan" debate concentrates; LFI also stretches the label into new domains (Legrain 2022 on education), a sign of formula productivity. Suggest, if wanted, spinning "choc d'offre (logement)" off as its own phrase — it is a distinct 2017-2018 government housing formula with its own genealogy, not noise.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2017-06-28 — Christian Jacob (LR), séance publique (installation du Bureau): "un vrai déni de démocratie et un irrespect du pluralisme politique" — the very first weeks of the corpus, protesting the opposition's exclusion from Bureau posts.
birth_notes: The expected birth (2023-03-16, 49.3 retraites) is NOT a birth in this corpus. "Déni de démocratie" is a pre-existing, fully cross-partisan parliamentary idiom present from the corpus's opening session (Jacob, LR, 2017-06-28) and used continuously 2017-2023 by LR, PS, GDR, LFI, MoDem, RN — and already quoted-and-rebutted by government ministers in 2017-2018 (Lemoyne on CETA: "aucun déni de démocratie"; Darmanin: "vous criez au déni de démocratie"). The 2018 Molac quotes tie it to the older 2005-referendum/2008-Lisbon-treaty grievance, confirming the formula predates 2017 entirely. March 2023 should be read as a frequency spike + re-anchoring onto the 49.3 (visible in de Courson 2023-03-20 doubling it: "déni de démocratie politique... déni de démocratie sociale"), not a lexical birth. The 2017-2022 material is genuine linguistic background, and the analysis should use the frequency curve, not first occurrence, as the 2023 signal.
notes: Zero false positives in 45 quotes — "déni" + "démocratie" has no literal/other-sense competitor, so the empty exclude list is correct and both patterns are safe. Striking features for the analysis: (1) the formula is meta-discursively contested — a large share of matches are the majority/government quoting it to rebut it (Lemoyne 2017, Darmanin 2018, Molac 2018, Cazenave 2024, Mazars 2025), so speaker counts should distinguish sincere accusation vs quotation/rebuttal; (2) the "déni démocratique" variant rises sharply post-2023 (Laisney, Maudet, Castor, JO-2030 quotes); (3) post-2023 targets broaden beyond the 49.3: JO 2030 (2025-12/2026-01), Nouvelle-Calédonie corps électoral — where Vigier (MoDem, 2026-05-20) inverts the formula, calling the frozen electorate itself "un déni démocratique intenable"; (4) accusers span every family including RN and government-side speakers, making this a shared-arsenal phrase rather than one camp's talking point. Minor optional recall note (no fix needed): the plural "dénis démocratiques" would be missed by the pattern "deni democratique" (could be "denis? democratiques?"), but the form is rare and adding it risks nothing either way.

fp_examples: Certains proviseurs m’ont rapporté que, lorsqu’ils prennent une mesure disciplinaire à l’encontre d’un élève multirécidiviste dans la bordélisation de sa classe ou de la cour de récréation, il arrive que les parents débarquent dans la minute pour contester la sanction.
true_birth_in_corpus: 2023-01-30, Sophie Taillé-Polian (Écologistes, commission des affaires sociales) — first genuine talking-point occurrence, a reversal of Darmanin's 2023-01-28 formula two days after it ("on sait qui bordélise : le Gouvernement"). Sincere anti-LFI adoption follows the next day: 2023-01-31 Thomas Ménagé (RN). Notable pre-birth parliamentary antecedent of the exact anti-LFI frame: 2020-02-12, Brigitte Bourguignon (majority) to Ugo Bernalicis — "Je ne sais pas qui bordélise le débat, monsieur Bernalicis" — after Bernalicis (LFI) himself used the verb minutes earlier, in the commission spéciale on the retraites reform.
birth_notes: Expected birth (Darmanin 2023-01-28, press) holds as the crystallization point — corpus density explodes from 2023-01-30 during the retraites PLFRSS battle. But the corpus reveals: (1) ordinary colloquial background pre-birth: Stéphane Peu (GDR) 2018 uses «bordélisent» in scare quotes about tenants messing up a building; (2) a striking proto-political antecedent 3 years before Darmanin: the 2020-02-12 Bernalicis↔Bourguignon exchange in the retraites commission spéciale already stages the exact "who bordélise the debate" duel between LFI and the majority — and the LFI deputy used the verb FIRST; Schellenberger (LR) 2021 "bordéliser la séance" extends this background. Darmanin's 2023 innovation was the nominalization ("bordélisation") and the scaling from "le débat/la séance" to "le pays/l'Assemblée". (3) In the AN itself, the formula is introduced post-birth by the OPPOSITION quoting-to-reverse it (Taillé-Polian, Écolos, Jan 30; then LFI: Panot Feb 6 quoting Véran/Borne, Vannier Feb 8 "Vous bordélisez le pays!" and the coinage "bordélisateurs"), with RN adopting it sincerely (Ménagé Jan 31) before LR (Bazin Feb 8) and Renaissance (Yadan Feb 17).
notes: Pattern `bordelis\w*` is near-perfect: 34/35 genuine (~97%), 1 false positive (~3%, school-discipline sense, itself plausibly downstream of the political neologism) — no fixes warranted; exclude list can stay empty. The stem catches the full derivational family (bordélise/bordélisent/bordéliser/bordélisiez/bordélisation/bordélisateurs). Striking analysis angles: (a) it is a boomerang formula — every camp wields it against another: executive/RN/LR vs LFI, LFI reversing at government and later at RN/UDR (Panot 2026-06-25 "le groupe UDR est en train de bordéliser sa propre niche"), Renaissance's Thevenot turning it on RN (2024-10-31), a Socialist heckling a colleague by first name (Echaniz 2025-03-12); (b) RN is the heaviest sincere adopter over time (Ménagé, Villedieu "doctrine de la bordélisation permanente", Bigot, Mauvieux, Falcon, Michoux/UDR); (c) meta-linguistic play confirms formula status: Corbière's "bordélisation → bardellisation" pun (2024-10-31; the pun itself would not match the pattern but co-occurs), Lucas-Lundy's ironic "sans bordélisation excessive" (2026); (d) by 2025 it is routinized into chair language and even self-defense ("Non, je ne bordélise pas", 2025-05-21). Corpus-quality flags, not pattern issues: the 2025-05-06 Bonnivard QAG quote appears twice (once as "(non identifié)"/Séance publique, once attributed under organ french_national_assembly) — dedup issue; and 2025+ entries include garbled ASR-style transcripts with unidentified speakers (e.g. 2025-05-21 "vous vous bordélisez cette Le rapporteur s'il vous plaît").

true_birth_in_corpus: 2021-03-15 — Naïma Moutchou (Gouvernement, commission spéciale climat): "dans le cadre d'une sorte d'arc républicain" — a hedged, inclusive coalition-building precursor. The first occurrence in the modern exclusionary talking-point sense (RN outside the arc) is 2022-12-14 — Bruno Millienne (MoDem, commission développement durable), seven months BEFORE Élisabeth Borne's expected 2023-07-04 relance.
birth_notes: The corpus contradicts the "Borne 2023-07-04" birth story in two ways. (1) Genuine pre-birth parliamentary use exists: Moutchou (March 2021, twice — note: identical sentence on 2021-03-15 and 2021-03-18, either a re-proposed amendment defense across "suite" sessions or a transcript duplication worth deduping) uses it in the INCLUSIVE sense (transpartisan amendment coalition), still hedged as "une sorte d'arc républicain" — the formula not yet crystallized. (2) The exclusionary sense was already an AN weapon before Borne: Millienne (MoDem) opposes "l'arc républicain" to the RN in Dec 2022, and by May 2023 Laisney (LFI) protests that a minister excluded LFI from working groups "au motif qu'il se situerait en dehors de « l'arc républicain »" — with scare quotes, showing the formula was already contested government vocabulary two months before the famous relance. Borne's role is amplification, not invention: Rudigoz (2023-07-19) explicitly attributes it to her ("comme l'a si bien rappelé Mme la Première ministre"), and frequency visibly explodes only after mid-2023 (3 matches 2021-2022 vs a flood from late 2023).
notes: Pattern quality is excellent: "arcs? republicains?" is self-disambiguating in a parliamentary corpus — no literal-sense competitors (bows/arches) appeared in any of the 35 unique quotes (15 first + 30 samples, 10 overlapping). Empty exclude list is justified; no fixes needed. Striking analysis material: the phrase is a boundary-drawing device fired in every direction — majority/left use it to expel the RN (Millienne, Léaument, Duplessy, Bernalicis), government used it against LFI (Laisney May 2023), LR against LFI (Ciotti), and LFI mirrors it back ironically (Coulomme "nous accuser de vos propres turpitudes", Obono, Martin). From 2023-11 onward the RN systematically reappropriates it — sincerely claiming membership (Bordes: "L'arc républicain que nous représentons"), redefining it (Bentz: "défini par les Français, pas par la rapporteure", Diaz invoking "11 millions d'électeurs"), or mocking it (Mauvieux: "le désormais célèbre arc républicain... n'a toujours fait que tirer des flèches contre sa propre nation" — even punning on arc/flèches). Post-2024-dissolution it shifts BACK toward the inclusive coalition sense among centrists seeking governing majorities (Molac, Latombe, de Courson, Potier, Chatelain). By 2025-2026 it drifts beyond party boundaries entirely: applied to newspapers (JDD/L'Humanité/Le Monde "appartiennent à l'arc républicain", 2026-01-22 audiovisual CE) and used by a non-politician CEO in a commission d'enquête (Salle, 2026-04-17) — the talking point has become common vocabulary. Venue pattern: early life in commissions (2021-2023), then séance publique after the July 2023 mainstreaming.

fp_examples: L'amélioration sera loin d'être négligeable : nous aurons un socle commun et des disciplines de spécialité, qui se partagent les heures à peu près par moitié – hormis les heures d'option dont je parlerai dans un instant. (Jean-Michel Blanquer, 2018-02-20 — socle commun du lycée, sens scolaire non capté par les exclusions)

L'amendement est satisfait, l'article L. 122-1-1 du code de l'éducation prévoyant déjà que l'acquisition du socle commun est progressive, en fonction des besoins particuliers de chaque enfant, de ses difficultés, de ses fragilités. (Anne-Christine Lang, 2019-01-30 — sens scolaire statutaire)

L'absence d'un socle commun de données qui permet de fonder des constats objectivés est problématique de mon point de vue. (Stéphane Delautrette, 2026-07-01 — idiome générique POST-naissance qui contamine le comptage politique)

pattern_fixes.detail: Replace `socles? communs?` with `(?<!un )socle commun(?! d[e'])`. The coalition sense is always definite and complement-free (le/du/au socle commun, ex-socle commun, « socle commun »), while generic idiom is 'un socle commun ...' or 'socle commun de/d'/des X' (droits, mesures, règles, données, services) — a construction that continues after 2024 and swamps the signal. Also drops the plural: 'socles communs' occurs only in generic use ('l'un des socles communs de la civilisation'). Verified against all sampled quotes: keeps 17/17 genuine political matches, kills all 4 post-birth generic FPs and most pre-birth generic noise (incl. 'aucun socle commun' via the lookbehind).

lycee — the 2018 Blanquer bac-reform hearings discuss the lycée 'socle commun' without any of the current exclude words (kills 'tous les lycées de France puissent proposer le même socle commun'); genuine coalition quotes never co-occur with 'lycée' in the sample.

mathematiques — curriculum debates about the socle commun mention maths ('l'exclusion des mathématiques de ce socle commun'; 'cours de mathématiques au titre de l'enseignement du socle commun'), coalition politics never does.

specialite — bac-reform vocabulary 'discipline(s) de spécialité' co-occurs with the curriculum socle commun ('abordés en socle commun ou dans une discipline de spécialité'); zero risk to political usage.
enseignement du socle — surgical phrase for 'l'enseignement du socle commun' (school sense with definite article, not caught by the tightened pattern).
acquisition du socle — surgical phrase for statutory school usage 'l'acquisition du socle commun est progressive' (code de l'éducation), which the tightened pattern alone does not catch.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2024-10-31 — Julie Laernoes (Écologistes), séance publique: "Je m'adresse maintenant aux collègues de droite du socle commun" — first genuine coalition-sense occurrence among sampled quotes. Caveat: the first-15 list only covers 2017-2018 pre-birth background, so the exact first political occurrence is not observable in this audit sample; re-extract first-15 after applying the pattern fixes to pin down the true parliamentary birth (likely Sept-Oct 2024, after Barnier's appointment).
birth_notes: The expected birth (Édouard Philippe, 2024-08-29) is plausible but not directly verifiable in the corpus: the AN was not sitting in late August 2024, and the first-15 raw matches (2017-07 to 2018-05) are entirely pre-birth material — 8 ordinary-idiom background uses ('un socle commun de droits' Fiat/Quatennens 2017-07-05, 'socles communs de la civilisation européenne' Nyssen) and 7 school-sense false positives from the Blanquer bac-reform hearings. The pre-2024 idiomatic baseline is heavy and continuous (2017-2023: droits des salariés, retraites, PAC, France Travail...), which is exactly why the coalition name felt bland and available. First sampled political use is by an OPPONENT (Laernoes, Écologistes, 2024-10-31), not the executive: in parliament the formula is overwhelmingly a hostile designator used by SOC/LFI/Éco/RN against the bloc (Le Pen 2024-12-04: 'votre soi-disant « socle commun »'), with only one sampled in-bloc use (Lalanne, Renaissance, 2026-05-12). So the corpus genealogy is: executive self-description coined outside the hemicycle, immediately captured by the opposition as a derisive label.
notes: FP rate 13/43 (~30%) across all judged quotes — well above the 10% threshold, hence verdict=fix. Two distinct contamination families: (a) the éducation-nationale 'socle commun' (the sense expected_birth explicitly says should be excluded) leaking past the exclude list via lycée/spécialité/mathématiques vocabulary — 9 quotes; (b) the ordinary idiom 'un socle commun de X' continuing AFTER the political birth (mesures 2024-10-22, règles 2025-12-11, qualité 2026-06-03, données 2026-07-01) — 4 quotes. Pre-birth ordinary-idiom uses (12) were counted GENUINE as linguistic background per instructions, but note the tightened pattern intentionally sacrifices most of that baseline to get a clean post-2024 political signal — for this phrase the political sense is a proper-noun-like coinage (a coalition NAME), syntactically separable (definite article, no 'de X' complement), so the trade-off is favorable and was verified quote-by-quote (17/17 genuine kept). Striking analysis angles: near-exclusive opposition usage; irony/quotation marks (soi-disant « socle commun »); and a traceable death of the formula from late 2025 ('feu le socle commun', 'l'ex-socle commun', 'socle commun ou du moins ce qu'il en reste'). One residual FP survives the fixes (Chudeau RN 2026-05-26, school sense with no distinctive vocabulary) — acceptable residue. Two random samples duplicated first-15 entries; counts are deduplicated (43 unique).

fp_examples: Il n’est pas intangible : on peut le simplifier, le rendre plus intelligent et plus flexible, comme je m’y suis efforcé, par un effort d’interprétation.
S’agissant des dispositions de la proposition, le plancher de peine que nous prévoyons ne sera pas intangible.
Notre modèle social est précieux, mais il n’est pas intangible.

pattern_fixes.detail: pas intangible — anchor-free and the sole source of all 4 false positives (2018 Moscovici on the stability pact, peines plancher, modèle social, Piketty on property); every genuine quote it caught except one is already covered by the anchored pattern, and that one is rescued by the widened window below

replace etat de droit[^.]{0,40}(intangible|sacre) with etat de droit[^.]{0,90}\b(intangible|sacre) — the 40-char window misses the genuine 2025-06-03 quote ('l'etat de droit dont le president du parti politique dit qu'il n'est pas intangible', gap ~57 chars); the \b guard prevents the wider window from matching 'sacre' inside 'consacre'/'massacre' ('l'etat de droit consacre par...' is a common phrasing on normalized text)

ni sacre,? ni intangible — the reversed order is frequent (Sebaihi 2025-06-17, Ménagé 2026-05-13, Coulomme 2026-06-25) and distinctive enough to be safe without the etat-de-droit anchor, covering paraphrases where the anchor drifts out of window

true_birth_in_corpus: 2024-10-02, Florent Boudié (Renaissance), commission des lois — opening the audition of Bruno Retailleau: « votre formule "l'État de droit n'est ni intangible, ni sacré" a suscité de très nombreuses réactions »
birth_notes: The corpus birth matches the expected story almost exactly: Retailleau's JDD formula of 2024-09-29 enters the Assemblée three days later (2024-10-02) at his own commission-des-lois audition, where six speakers from five groups (Renaissance, LFI, Écologistes, GDR, PS) quote it at him in a single day — a burst birth, not a gradual adoption. There is no genuine pre-birth background of the formula: the lone pre-2024 raw match (2018-05-24, unidentified speaker at the Moscovici audition) is generic 'pas intangible' about EU budget rules, i.e. a pattern artifact, not the idiom's baseline. Crucially, the formula's author never utters it in the corpus sample — every genuine occurrence is oppositional: quotation-as-indictment (LFI, PS, GDR, Écologistes, and even Renaissance's Boudié) or explicit rebuttal inverting the terms ('l'État de droit est quant à lui intangible', 'a un caractère sacré', Boudié's 'République de sable'). One RN deputy (Ménagé, 2026-05-13) also cites it warily against Retailleau-as-candidate. Venue pattern: born in commission (Lois), then migrates to séance publique via the censure motions of 2025-01-16 and 2025-02-19, then QAG; still alive in June 2026 in presidential-campaign framing. This is a negative-genealogy talking point: coined outside parliament, living inside it exclusively as a weapon against its author.
notes: Counts are over 26 unique quotes (15 first-in-corpus + 11 non-duplicate random samples; the other random samples repeat the first-15 verbatim). FP rate 4/26 ≈ 15%, all from the loose 'pas intangible' pattern — hence verdict fix rather than keep. The ambiguous case is Bernalicis 2026-04-28, a garbled oral transcript that gestures at the controversy but cannot be confidently parsed. Pattern 2 ('ni intangible,? ni sacre') produced only genuine hits. After the proposed fixes, every genuine quote in the sample remains matched: verified per-quote against the anchored 90-char pattern, pattern 2, or the new reversed-order pattern.

true_birth_in_corpus: 2025-03-31 — Julien Limongi (RN), séance publique: "la démocratie est victime d'une dictature des juges" (with recorded protests); same day Frédéric Falcon (RN) uses "gouvernement des juges" twice about the Le Pen conviction. This is the first genuine occurrence of the escalated post-condemnation talking point. Background family usage starts 2018-06-06 (Marie-France Lorho, RN, commission des lois).
birth_notes: Corpus confirms the successor structure. "Gouvernement des juges" (Lambert, 1921) runs as continuous cross-spectrum juristic background from 2018-06-06 (first: Marie-France Lorho, RN) through 2024 — RN, Renaissance, Horizons, LR, MoDem all use it, almost only in commissions, often hedged ("je sais que le terme paraîtra outrancier" — Gosselin 2021). The genuinely new 2025 element is the lexical escalation and venue shift: "dictature des juges" debuts 2025-03-31 (Limongi, RN, séance publique) — the same day as Bardella's expected public birth; Bardella is not an AN speaker, so RN deputies (Limongi, Falcon) carried the talking point into the hemicycle. Striking: the exact canonical phrase "tyrannie des juges" first appears in-corpus on 2025-04-01 from an OPPONENT, Sandrine Runel (Socialistes), denying it ("Il n'y a pas de justice politique ni de tyrannie des juges") — the formula enters the record as a rebuttal. Post-birth, left/center uses are mostly ironic or rebuttals (Fesneau "Et après, on dira que c'est la république des juges !", Kerbrat's "gouvernement des juges rouges" echoing the 1970s "juges rouges" trope, Leseul 2026).
notes: Zero false positives — the phrase family has no literal other sense, so patterns are safe as-is; empty exclude list is correct. "tyrannie judiciaire" produced no visible matches but is harmless. 12 of the 30 random samples duplicate first-15 entries (33 unique quotes total). Genuine uses split three ways, all analytically valuable: sincere invocation (right/RN), rebuttal/debate-about-the-formula (Boudié 2018, Thillaye 2021, Dupond-Moretti 2021, Runel 2025), and irony/quotation (Fesneau, Kerbrat). Venue is a clean birth signal: pre-2025 = commission/constitutional-law register; from 2025-03-31 = séance publique/militant with protest reactions. Data-quality asides: Julien Aubert's quote of 2021-03-15 and 2021-03-18 is word-identical across two session ids (possible duplicated transcript), and party metadata contains the typo "Les Récuplicains".

true_birth_in_corpus: 2017-07-04 — Laurence Dumont (Socialistes), commission des affaires sociales, on the loi d'habilitation for the ordonnances travail ("ce passage en force et cet abaissement du Parlement"); Jean-Luc Mélenchon uses "passer en force" in séance publique the same day. This is the corpus start, not a birth — the idiom clearly predates the corpus.
birth_notes: The expected-birth entry (2023-03-16, "(idiome ancien)", role=baseline) is confirmed by the corpus: "passage en force" is fully formed political vocabulary from the very first weeks of the 2017 legislature — opposition accusation (Dumont, Mélenchon 2017-07-04; Fiat, Manin 2018) AND government/majority denial (Pompili "bien loin du « passage en force » que certains dénoncent" 2018-04-03; Rossi, Le Maire, Moutchou 2018) both appear within the first 15 matches. The 2023 pension-49.3 moment is an amplification peak, not a birth: the explicit "passage en force + 49.3" pairing shows up in samples (Lucas-Lundy 2023-05-31; budget debate 2026-01-15). Post-May-2024 the phrase migrates heavily onto the New Caledonia dossier (dégel du corps électoral, référendum, accord de Bougival): ~10 of the 2024-2026 samples are Nouvelle-Calédonie. So the phrase works exactly as intended as a baseline étalon of procedural-crisis background noise.
notes: All 45 quotes (15 first + 30 samples) are the political imposition sense; zero literal/sports false positives — the sports "passage en force" never appears in AN debate transcripts. One AMBIGUOUS: 2025-05-21 Défense commission ("certains pourront dire qu'on peut passer en force... ça se paye") could be the military sense of forcing a passage. Sociology: accusers are LFI/Écologistes/Socialistes/GDR/LR-in-opposition; Renaissance/Gouvernement speakers use it almost exclusively in denial or scare-quoted meta form ("il n'y a pas de passage en force" Genevard 2026; "j'entends l'expression de passage en force" Galliard-Minier 2026) — the accusation/denial dialectic is itself part of the formula's life. Data quirk: the 2025-10-22 quote "Nous avons donc là la légitimité du droit... aucun passage en force" appears twice verbatim in random_samples (duplicate corpus row or sampling artifact) — worth a dedup check upstream. Optional recall improvement (NOT needed for FP hygiene, and changing it would alter baseline counts uniformly): current patterns miss conjugated/participle forms ("est passee en force", "passes en force", "en passant en force", "vous passez en force", "passeront en force"); an FP-safe add_pattern on normalized text would be "pass(?:es|ez|ees?|ait|aient|ant|eront|ons) en force". Keeping patterns as-is is fine since a baseline phrase mainly needs temporally consistent measurement.
